trim_reasons: SAS is favored by 10.5 — a comfortable blowout-risk spread where Fox's minutes floor drops to 22.8 (avg 28.3), meaning Q4 garbage-time rest is plausible. If SAS pulls ahead early, Fox's assist opportunities compress precisely when he'd need volume to clear T4.

VOLATILE tag on a blowout-risk game (spread -10.5) means Fox's 3PM distribution is wide, and a comfortable SAS lead could reduce his shot attempts in Q4. The 3PM floor is thin — one off-shooting night where he goes 0-for-2 from deep in reduced minutes is well within the miss band for a volatile player.

Wemby is QUES with a VOLATILE PTS tag and a post_miss lift of only 0.80 (below 1.0), indicating bounce-back is not reliable. Combined with blowout risk (SAS -10.5) and a min_floor of 25.0 on a QUES player, a precautionary minutes restriction — even 4–5 fewer minutes — could push him below T15 on a night his shot isn't falling.

VOLATILE tag on AST with a massive 23.5-point spread — even on the winning side, historical audit data confirms blowout AST suppression is real for non-primary creators (see NAW lesson). Barnes as a PF/SF in a comfortable TOR win may see creation sequences collapse in Q4, compressing his assist volume despite full minutes.

min_floor=0.0 signals recent DNPs — Ingram's availability is fragile, and even if he plays, the blowout spread of -23.5 creates strong Q4 rest risk. A player with DNP history on a massive blowout-risk game has the most compressed minutes floor of any pick on this slate.

min_floor=0.0 (confirmed DNPs in recent games) combined with a spread of -23.5 and post_miss_hr of only 67% (lift 0.75, the weakest bounce-back on the slate) makes this structurally fragile. The blowout cap was applied at 74%, but the DNP risk means even playing time is uncertain, and the winning-blowout AST suppression mechanism documented in audit history adds a second independent risk layer that the confidence adjustment does not fully price.

vs_tough=79% is materially below the 90% overall rate (11pp gap), and NYK is a tough defense on the road with CHA as an underdog in a 13.5-point spread game. The combination of tough defense suppression plus blowout risk (away team in a potential blowout gets minutes compressed) creates a two-sided squeeze on this floor.

audit history explicitly documents that Miller posted 0 assists in 31 minutes against a tough defense despite scoring 22 points — iron-floor tags can still produce zero-assist games. The BLOWOUT_RISK at spread=13.5 on the road adds winning-side AST sequence collapse risk, and trend=down was flagged in the pick_reasoning, meaning the structural fragility here is documented and repeated.

post_miss_hr=57% (lift=0.69) is the weakest bounce-back profile on the slate — when Bridges misses, he slumps sequentially. Combined with vs_tough=76% (14pp below overall) against a NYK tough defense on the road, the floor here is materially softer than the 90% headline hit rate suggests for this specific game context.

At spread_abs=15.5, Edgecombe is flagged as a secondary scorer in a blowout-risk game — the Bane lesson at 14.5 is directly precedent-setting here, as secondary scorers in large blowouts see minutes compressed even when the hard-skip threshold doesn't formally fire. The system applied a penalty, but the structural mechanism (secondary scorer pulled in Q4 garbage time) remains live.

vs_tough=71% is 19pp below the 90% overall rate — the largest vs_tough suppression gap for an AST pick on this slate. ATL is a tough defensive opponent, and Adebayo's AST generation is demonstrably vulnerable in these matchups; the post_miss_hr of 82% with a typical miss of only 1 point below threshold offers thin margin when the suppression mechanism fires.

JJ is QUES with a VOLATILE PTS tag, FG_COLD flag, and a post_miss_hr of 79% (lift 0.97, below 1.0) indicating bounce-back is not reliable. A QUES player with a cold-shooting trend and volatile distribution faces real DNP or restricted-minutes risk that the 70% confidence does not fully buffer against.

post_miss_hr=61% (lift=0.82) is structurally weak — when JJ misses his AST floor, he tends to slump sequentially. As a SF/PF non-primary creator who is also QUES, any minutes restriction compresses his assist-generation sequences, and the 61% post-miss rate signals this prop has a genuine floor problem under adverse conditions.

VOLATILE tag with FG_COLD:-16% (severe, triggering mandatory step-down) means Garland is in a confirmed cold-shooting stretch. Even at T10, a volatile player in active FG_COLD suppression has a wide distribution where sub-10 scoring nights are meaningfully probable, particularly if the cold-shooting pattern persists.

LAL is favored by 14.5 with BLOWOUT_RISK — audit history confirms that winning-side blowouts suppress AST creation sequences even with full minutes, and LeBron's T6 floor (not T4, which was unavailable) sits meaningfully higher than his natural iron-floor tier. A comfortable LAL lead reducing late-game playmaking urgency could compress assist opportunities below T6.

The Bane blowout lesson is directly applicable here: ORL is favored by 13.5 vs BOS, Bane is a secondary scorer, and the audit explicitly documents that spread_abs=14.5 produced a catastrophic miss (2 actual points, 17 minutes played). At 13.5 the hard-skip threshold does not fire, but the structural mechanism — secondary scorer in a large ORL blowout win gets pulled — is identical to the documented miss. The system stepped down and capped confidence, but the underlying minutes-compression risk for Bane in an ORL blowout remains fully live and has already produced the worst single-pick miss in recent audit history.

Same blowout mechanism applies to 3PM — if Bane plays only 17–20 minutes as documented in the prior ORL blowout, he may attempt only 2–3 threes, making OVER 1 contingent on volume that a garbage-time exit eliminates. The iron-floor held for Bane AST in a prior blowout, but 3PM without iron-floor protection is more fragile.
